title: Authentication, Authorization, RBAC, Service Accounts, Tokens, and Impersonation · Control-Plane Trace: K8s Token Audience Rotation Review
summary: A CI workload uses the default service account and a long-lived token while a broad ClusterRoleBinding permits secret reads and workload creation across tenants. Planned artifact: Add and verify the k8s token audience rotation review increment in least-privilege identity and authorization policy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Use TokenRequest and projected bound tokens with audience, expiry, rotation, revocation, automount controls, authorization checks, and periodic review.
Detect bind, escalate, impersonate, service account, secret, workload-creation, node-proxy, CSR, aggregation, and wildcard privilege escalation paths.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never invoke kubectl, read kubeconfig, contact a cluster API, or execute host commands.
workspace: kubernetes
version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster
evidence:

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: A worked example workload uses the default ServiceAccount, automounts a broad token, and receives wildcard cluster-admin through a group binding. Derive subject, verb, resource, namespace, token audience, impersonation, and least-privilege proof. Competency probe: Use TokenRequest and projected bound tokens with audience, expiry, rotation, revocation, automount controls, authorization checks, and periodic review. A defensible solution applies K8s Token Audience Rotation Review, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to least-privilege identity and authorization policy.

content.code:
# Evidence: k8s-worked-k8s-identity-rbac-serviceaccounts-2
  - competency: k8s-worked-k8s-identity-rbac-serviceaccounts-2
    artifact: rbac-policy.yaml
    decision: worked k8s identity rbac serviceaccounts 2
    environment: rootless development cluster with an empty namespace
    fault-injection: make an admission dependency unavailable during an upgrade
    retained-proof: accessible incident timeline with causal regression evidence
    case-key: worked2
    change: |
      serviceAccount: intake/status-api
      automountServiceAccountToken: false
      token: projected-audience-intake-api
      role: get-configmap-status-only
      binding: namespace-intake
      review: auth-can-i-and-escalation-map
    verify: kubectl auth can-i --list --as system:serviceaccount:intake:status-api -n intake
    failure: Reject broad ClusterRoleBinding, long-lived static tokens, wildcard verbs, secret reads, bind or escalate paths, or workload creation as harmless.
    repair: Separate human and workload identity, scope verbs and resources, use bound audience tokens, test authorization, and review escalation periodically.
content.caption: Runnable Kubernetes object and transfer-evidence contract example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: A projected service account token is valid forever and for every service that trusts the cluster issuer. This fails the K8s Token Audience Rotation Review contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.
